Idiom (Cham): Avêr la têta dens las nûvlas
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal expression with infinitive ‘avêr’ + definite noun phrase; metaphorical locative structure
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To be distracted, unrealistic, or lost in one's thoughts instead of paying attention to reality
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Être distrêt o pas concentrâ sur la situacion
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Pastoral imagery from Alpine life where clouds symbolized distance and dreaminess
First Example Sentence: Arrêta d’avêr la têta dens las nûvlas et escota lo professor.
Second Example Sentence: Il avêt la têta dens las nûvlas pendent tota la reunion.
Third Example Sentence: Se te gardes la têta dens las nûvlas, tu feras d’errors.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Transitive verb + definite object; causative idiomatic construction
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To make someone eager or curious by giving only partial information
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Susitar la curiositâ sens donar totas las informacions
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Linked to traditional food culture where anticipation before meals was emphasized
First Example Sentence: La publicitâ met l’aiga a la boca sens revelar lo produït.
Second Example Sentence: Il a metû l’aiga a la boca avouéc son istouèra misteriosa.
Third Example Sentence: Ne mete pas l’aiga a la boca se tu ne veux pas explicar.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Reflexive verb phrase; possessive adjective + noun; metaphorical reversal
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To change sides or loyalties for personal advantage
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Changar d’opinion o de camp por interêt personal
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: From rural clothing imagery where turning a jacket inside out symbolized opportunism
First Example Sentence: En politica, qualques uns tornon lor jaca rapidament.
Second Example Sentence: Il a tornâ sa jaca quand lo vent a changiâ.
Third Example Sentence: Ne torna pas ta jaca solament por profit.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb ‘avêr’ + definite noun + prepositional phrase; metaphor of physical possession
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To be emotionally vulnerable or sincere
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in troubadour lyrical traditions of the Arpitan-speaking regions
First Example Sentence: Ela parlâvêt avouéc lo còr en man.
Second Example Sentence: Quand il s’excusa, il avêt lo còr en man.
Third Example Sentence: Por escrîre poesia, falyêt avêr lo còr en man.

Idiom (Cham): Fêr montâ la mostarda al nas
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Causative verb phrase; culinary metaphor with prepositional complement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: From regional mustard production in Burgundy and Savoy areas
First Example Sentence: Sa remarça m’a fait montâ la mostarda al nas.
Second Example Sentence: Ne me fês pas montâ la mostarda al nas avouéc tos reproches.
Third Example Sentence: Il suffit d’un mot por me fêr montâ la mostarda al nas.

Idiom (Cham): Être coma lo vent de bise
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To be cold, distant, or emotionally harsh
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Refers to the cold northern wind (bise) common around Lake Léman
First Example Sentence: Son regârd est coma lo vent de bise.
Second Example Sentence: Ela foguèt coma lo vent de bise pendent l’entrevista.
Third Example Sentence: Ne siês pas coma lo vent de bise avouéc tos amîs.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase with prepositional complement; delicate action metaphor
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To act cautiously in a sensitive situation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from rural life where stepping on eggs implied extreme care
First Example Sentence: En negociacion, nos marchens sur d’ûes.
Second Example Sentence: Il marcha sur d’ûes por evitar lo conflic.
Third Example Sentence: Se tu marches sur d’ûes, tot se passarâ ben.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase with relative clause; physiological metaphor
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Traditional metaphor linking blood and temperament in Alpine folklore
First Example Sentence: Quand i penso a ceti injustiça, i avô lo sang que bulh.
Second Example Sentence: Son sang bulhâ quand il ausiâ la critica.
Third Example Sentence: Ne parle pas pus, sinon mon sang bulhira.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Transitive verb phrase; culinary metaphor; fixed plural noun
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To resolve a difficult situation for others who benefit
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Shared Romance idiom tied to chestnut harvesting traditions in mountain regions
First Example Sentence: Il a tirâ las castanhas del fôc por tota l’equipa.
Second Example Sentence: Ne siês pas totjorn cel que tira las castanhas del fôc.
Third Example Sentence: En crisi, qualqu’un devent tirar las castanhas del fôc.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase; descriptive adjective modifying past participle; metaphor of looseness
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To be talkative or unable to keep secrets
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Oral storytelling traditions in village gatherings
First Example Sentence: Ela a la lenga ben pendûa et conta tot.
Second Example Sentence: Se tu as la lenga ben pendûa, gardâ un secrèt serà dificil.
Third Example Sentence: Il rît e parla, avouéc la lenga ben pendûa.
